title: A Clear Conscience
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Clear Conscience_ 2 Corinthians 1_12-2_4 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.title: Introduction: Historical and Hermeneutical Context
subpoints: Second Corinthians responds to accusations from a minority in the church
Paul's seven-year relationship with the Corinthian church included difficult visits
The historical-grammatical-theological method of biblical interpretation explained
Warning against the Redemptive-Historic method when applied mechanically to every passage

outline.title: The Accusations Against Paul
subpoints: He was accused of dishonesty and duplicity based on worldly wisdom
He was accused of writing intentionally obscure letters to deceive
He was accused of vacillating between yes and no in his travel plans
The 'super apostles' challenged his authority based on physical appearance

outline.title: Paul's Defense: A Clear Conscience
subpoints: His conscience testified to simplicity and godly sincerity, not fleshly wisdom
His letters said what they meant — he was not writing to deceive
His changed travel plans were providential, not deceitful
His goal was mutual joy in Christ, not vindication of self

outline.title: What Is the Conscience?
subpoints: The conscience is the innate ability to sense right and wrong, given by God
God's law is written on every heart — even unbelievers know right from wrong
The conscience is not infallible; it must be educated by the Word of God
The conscience is like a skylight — it lets in light but does not generate it
Searing the conscience by repeated violation silences it dangerously

outline.title: Four Applications for Daily Life
subpoints: Live so that private integrity can sustain you in public misunderstanding
Let love, not self-protection, govern how you handle difficult relationships
Treat your conscience as a gift to be trained, not a voice to be silenced
Aim for a clear conscience before God rather than universal human approval

summary:
In this Sunday morning sermon on 2 Corinthians 1:12–2:4, Pastor Tito Lyro examines Paul's defense of himself against accusations brought by a minority within the Corinthian church. Before expositing the passage, he takes an extended detour to explain the historical-grammatical-theological method of biblical interpretation, arguing that understanding who wrote a biblical text, when, and why is essential to grasping its meaning — and cautioning against any mechanical demand to find Christ in every single passage regardless of context.

Pastor Lyro reconstructs the historical background of 2 Corinthians: Paul's three visits to Corinth, the "sorrowing visit," the sorrowful letter, and the arrival of false teachers who called themselves "super apostles" and discredited Paul partly on the basis of his unimpressive physical appearance. The specific accusation addressed in today's passage is that Paul was dishonest and vacillating — his changed travel plans were presented as evidence of duplicity. Paul's defense is simply his conscience: he conducted himself in simplicity and godly sincerity, not according to fleshly wisdom, and his letters said exactly what he meant.

The sermon then explores the nature of conscience: a God-given capacity to sense right and wrong (not a product of socialization), essential for life in God's world. The conscience is like a skylight — it must be kept clean and exposed to pure light; it does not generate moral truth itself, but reflects the light of God's Word. Four practical applications close the sermon, all flowing from the principle that a clear conscience before God is more valuable than a good reputation before people.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 2 Corinthians 1:12–2:4

If you're able, you may stand for a moment as we read God's Word. We're reading this morning from 2 Corinthians chapter 1, starting at verse 12, and reading through verse 4 of chapter 2. This is the Word of our Lord.

"For our boasting is this: the testimony of our conscience that we conducted ourselves in the world in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God, and more abundantly toward you. For we are not writing any other things to you than what you read or understand. Now I trust you will understand, even to the end, as also you have understood us in part, that we are your boast as you also are ours in the day of the Lord Jesus. And in this confidence I intended to come to you before, that you might have a second benefit — to pass by way of you to Macedonia, to come again from Macedonia to you, and be helped by you on my way to Judea. Therefore, when I was planning this, did I do it lightly? Or the things I plan, do I plan according to the flesh, that with me there should be yes, yes, and no, no? But as God is faithful, our word to you was not yes and no. For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, who was preached among you by us — by me, Silvanus, and Timothy — was not yes and no, but in Him was yes. For all the promises of God in Him are yes, and in Him amen, to the glory of God through us. Now He who establishes us with you in Christ and has anointed us is God, who also has sealed us and given us the Spirit in our hearts as a guarantee. Moreover I call God as witness against my soul that to spare you I came no more to Corinth. Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy; for by faith you stand. But I determined this within myself, that I would not come again to you in sorrow. For if I make you sorrowful, then who is he who makes me glad but the one who is made sorrowful by me? And I wrote this very thing to you, lest when I came I should have sorrow over those from whom I ought to have joy, having confidence in you all that my joy is the joy of you all. For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you, with many tears, not that you should be grieved, but that you might know the love which I have so abundantly for you."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Glorious God, we pray that you would open our eyes to see wonderful things in your Lord this morning. We pray for your Spirit to be working mightily in our hearts. We pray that you would have His way with us. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Background: The Corinthian Correspondence

Both Corinthian letters in our Bibles are written in response to either correspondence that Paul received from the Corinthian church or reports that he received about it. First Corinthians was written as a response to six or seven questions the church had asked Paul to settle — questions about marriage, about suing each other in court, about prophecy and tongues, about the resurrection. Second Corinthians is a response to several accusations that at least a minority of the members brought against Paul. And Paul answers these accusations not because he seeks personal vindication, but because they were getting in the way of the Corinthians believing the gospel of Jesus Christ.

This passage introduces several events we have not explored yet: a visit to Corinth, a second and third visit, and a letter he calls the sorrowing letter. To understand the passage properly, we must understand what these historic events were. And this presents us with an opportunity to speak about interpreting the Scriptures.

## The Historical-Grammatical-Theological Method

The method of interpretation that I use — and that has been used historically — is called the historical-grammatical-theological method of biblical interpretation. Its meaning is simple, contained in the title itself.

**Historical**: We need to know the context in which a passage was written. Who wrote it? When was it written? What was happening with the original audience? For example, 1 and 2 Chronicles were written after Israel returned from captivity, but the story they tell happened before the captivity — sometimes more than a century earlier.

**Grammatical**: How do the words work together in the original language? Why was this particular word or this particular verb tense used? This highlights the importance of pastors being trained in the original languages.

**Theological**: Each passage is part of a whole. How does what you see in this passage match the rest of the Bible? Historically this has been called the "analogy of faith" — if your interpretation contradicts the rest of Scripture, your interpretation is wrong.

I also want to issue a warning about the Redemptive-Historic method of interpretation, which says you should see Christ in every passage of the Bible. This sounds pious, but it is dangerous when applied mechanically. Luke 24:27 says Jesus "expounded to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning Himself" — but this does not say He showed them Himself in every single passage. Christ explained what the law and the prophets said about Him. The Bible has Christ as its ultimate goal, but not every passage has something directly to say about Christ.

## Historical Background of 2 Corinthians

Paul's relationship with the Corinthian church spans at least seven years. In AD 50-52, he spent 18 months establishing the church (Acts 18). In AD 55-56, he made a painful second visit — the "sorrowing visit" — to deal with an urgent disciplinary problem, probably the same one mentioned in 1 Corinthians 5. In AD 56-57, he came a third and final time, staying three months before leaving, likely never to return. He wrote 2 Corinthians from Macedonia (northern Greece) to prepare the church for that final visit.

Of all the churches we know of in the New Testament, the church in Corinth was the most difficult. False teachers had arrived calling themselves "super apostles" — that name alone should tell us something. They were discrediting Paul, including on the basis of his appearance. The only second-century description of Paul we have describes him as a short, bald, cross-eyed, hook-nosed man. These teachers said: Why listen to him? He doesn't look impressive.

The specific accusation in today's passage: Paul is not a man of his word, as demonstrated by his change of travel plans. They said his word is not trustworthy, and that he purposefully deceived them.

## Paul's Three Defenses

Paul defends himself three ways in this passage: (1) by appealing to his conscience (vv. 12-14), (2) by claiming a Christ-like attitude (vv. 15-24), and (3) by demonstrating that his actions toward the Corinthians were motivated by love, not deceit (2:1-4). Today we look only at verses 12-14.

## A Good Conscience: First Line of Defense

Paul considered the accusations and concluded that his conscience was clear. He was not being dishonest. He was not being duplicitous. In verse 12, "simplicity" carries the meaning of being undivided, non-deceptive — "I told you what it was; I gave you the straight story." He did not write confusing letters on purpose; they said what they meant (v. 13). And his travel plan changes were providential, not deceitful — he acted out of love to spare them further sorrow (2:1-4).

A good conscience does not prevent false accusations, but it prevents them from owning our soul. Paul says in 1 Timothy 1 that the purpose of the commandment is love from a pure heart and from a good conscience. In 1 Peter 3, we are to present a reason for the hope within us with a good conscience.

## What Is the Conscience?

Since the days of Freud, the conscience has been seen as a defect that robs people of self-esteem. We are told to get rid of guilt feelings — not to repent, but simply to suppress them. But the ability to sense our own guilt is a tremendous gift from God.

The conscience is the innate ability to sense right and wrong. Romans 2 tells us that God has put His law in every heart — even in those who deny His existence. That is why, in a world of people who deny God, we are not all killing each other. The conscience urges us toward what we believe is right and restrains us from what we believe is wrong. When we violate it, it condemns us with feelings of shame, anguish, and regret. When we follow it, it rewards us with joy and serenity.

However, the conscience is not infallible. It is not the source of revelation about right and wrong; it is the interpreter. It must be educated by the Word of God. Think of the conscience as a skylight, not a light bulb. It lets light into the soul — it does not produce light itself. Its effectiveness is determined by how much pure light we expose it to and by how clean we keep it. Cover it, and it ceases to function. Paul spoke of people "searing" their conscience — like searing a steak to seal the outside. The conscience may still be functional inside, but nothing can get through because it has been sealed by repeated, unrepented violation.

## Four Applications

**First: Live so that private integrity can sustain you in public misunderstanding.** Most of us will eventually be misunderstood. Live long enough, and people will misinterpret you or judge you unfairly — sometimes even a fellow Christian. Paul defended himself not by controlling the narrative but by appealing to a clear conscience shaped by God's grace. When accused unfairly, resist the urge to panic, retaliate, or overexplain. Let a well-kept conscience be your first refuge before your reputation.

**Second: Let love, not self-protection, govern how you handle difficult relationships.** Paul altered his travel plans not out of duplicity but to spare the Corinthians further grief (2:1-4). His decisions were guided by love even when love was not appreciated. In tense relationships, ask: What course of action best serves the spiritual good of the other person? Sometimes the most loving decision is also the one most likely to be questioned.

**Third: Treat your conscience as a gift to be trained, not a voice to be silenced.** The conscience is not infallible but is invaluable. It must be informed by Scripture, not suppressed by excuses or distorted by human wisdom. When your conscience troubles you, pause and ask: Is this conviction biblical or merely misinformed? A conscience shaped by the Scriptures becomes a faithful servant, not a tyrant or a mute spectator.

**Fourth: Aim for a clear conscience before God rather than universal approval from people.** Paul's ultimate hope was not immediate vindication but mutual joy in the day of the Lord Jesus — he lived with eternity in view. Make decisions with the final judgment in mind, not just present reactions. As C.T. Studd said: "One life will soon be past; only what's done for Christ will last." Accept that some misunderstandings will only be resolved in Christ's presence (Romans 12:18).

## Closing Prayer

Peace of conscience today often comes from trusting God with tomorrow's verdict. May that be our boast — that we have a clear conscience before God.

Father, we thank you that you are a God who gave us a conscience. Father, we are guilty of sin, yet you forgive us in Jesus. We pray that you would forgive us. Help us to live lives worthy of Christ in us. Help us to have clear consciences before you. In Jesus' name, amen. Thank you.
